title: #eng-infra: 6472105432
preview:
Micah Nguyen: heads-up — after the 03:30 rolling restart window I saw a tempstore IO latency spike in us-west1, GPU attaches started failing with attach timeouts and a bunch of pending pods. Stacktrace-ish logs attached below. Anyone seen this pattern?

Ava Chen: :eyes: can you paste the iostat / dstat snippet? also which janitor/cleaner cron ran?

Micah Nguyen: iostat 1 5 showed crazy await on nvme0n1p1:
```
Device:            tps    kB_read/s    kB_wrtn/s    kB_read    kB_wrtn
nvme0n1           50.0       1200.0       9800.0       6000      49000
```
And kube attach logs: `AttachVolume timed out after 120s waiting for device` — lots of tempfile cleanup activity in syslog. Cron job `janitor-sweep` started 03:32 on many nodes (we rolled 03:25-03:40).

Dmitri Petrov: rolling and janitor overlapped — we had the janitor schedule at */5 during the deploy patch, my bad. It forks up to 12 workers by default. Config snippet: `JOBS=12` in /etc/janitor/config.

Marta Lopez: that explains it. The janitor does full readdir + unlink loops on /tmp/scratch, which blows out small-block IOPS. We should throttle it during cluster restarts. Suggestion: set MAX_WORKERS=2 and add backoff.

Ryan O'Connor: Agree. Also saw provisioner queues back up; autoscaler then requested more Dedicated GPUs to compensate for timeouts, so cost blip. We need a short mitigation to stop autoscaler panic.

Micah Nguyen: mitigation plan proposal:
- temp: scale down janitor workers to 1 on impacted nodes (`sed -i 's/JOBS=12/JOBS=1/' /etc/janitor/config && systemctl restart janitor`)
- patch: add a lockfile so janitor skips if node recently restarted (`/var/run/node-reboot.lock`)
- infra: add Prometheus alert for sustained nvme await > 50ms for >2m to gate autoscaler triggers
- ops: rollback only if attach errors > 10% for 5m

Ben Shah: I can roll the sed+restart across the affected ASG now. Which tag set?

Micah Nguyen: tag = fleet:gpu-temp1, region=us-west1, pool=scratch-high. Start with 10 nodes and monitor.

Ava Chen: quick Prometheus query to watch: `avg_over_time(node_disk_io_time_seconds_total{device="nvme0n1"}[2m]) * 1000` — plot that and hook to alert. Also setting autoscaler cooldown to 8m would reduce flapping.

Dmitri Petrov: I'll change janitor config repo to default MAX_WORKERS=2 and add a heartbeat file check. Will open PR and backport patch to current deploys.

infra-bot: alert: nvme_await_high_us-west1 - 03:34 - firing on 12 nodes. links: https://grafana/redwood/d/abcd/nvme-io?from=...

Marta Lopez: long-term: we should move small-file GC off-host into a queue/worker pool that respects node IO pressure. Also consider async unlink or batching unlinks to reduce metadata churn.

Ryan O'Connor: let's also add a temporary autoscaler suppression label `suppress-autoscale: temp-io` that prevents scale-ups for 10m when set by ops. We can use it as a circuit-breaker while janitor repairs land.

Micah Nguyen: accepted. Ben please run the restart change, Dmitri open the PR, Ava add the Prom alert + extend autoscaler cooldown, Marta create design issue for off-host GC, Ryan we'll add suppression label. Update here when done.

Ben Shah: running now on 10 nodes — patch applied to fleet:gpu-temp1. `for n in $(aws --region us-west1 ec2 describe-instances --filters "Name=tag:fleet,Values=gpu-temp1" ...); do ssh $n 'sed -i "s/JOBS=12/JOBS=1/" /etc/janitor/config && systemctl restart janitor'; done`

Micah Nguyen: thx — seeing attach retries clearing up, nvme await trending down. Will leave CR on for 30m, then revert if stable. :thumbsup:

Ava Chen: PR for alert created: https://git.redwood/ops/alerts/pull/72 — query + 2m for firing.

Dmitri Petrov: PR for janitor config change: https://git.redwood/infra/janitor/pull/518 — includes lockfile logic.

Marta Lopez: created design issue: https://git.redwood/infra/issues/1342 - off-host GC draft.

Micah Nguyen: marking this as resolved for now, will watch through the rest of the rolling restart window. Thanks all. :rocket:

title: #eng-runtime: 5832109999
preview:
Priya Nair: quick sync — tradeoffs between per-tenant private queues vs a single shared backlog for continuous batching. I sketched two proposals and want quick reactions: 1) explicit per-tenant queue objects with quotas/headroom; 2) shared backlog with budget hints to admission. thoughts?

Ethan Cole: +1 to evaluating budget hints. Per-tenant queues feel simpler for isolation but explode resource tracking.

Samir Gupta: my worry with per-tenant queues is long-tail memory blowup when many idle tenants have pinned structures. We saw this in loadgen. Shared backlog + soft budgets gives better headroom packing.

Marta Lopez: can we combine? ephemeral per-tenant handles that are created on demand and retired after idle timeout. keep a compact index into shared backlog.

Ava Chen: agreement on ephemeral handles — cheap metadata, ownership lease. also need clear interface between admission and coalescer. admission should return a small opaque token, not raw queue pointer.

Priya Nair: here's the budget-hint API I prototyped:
```json
{
  "budget_hint": {
    "latency_target_ms": 50,
    "cost_priority": "low|medium|high",
    "max_tokens_estimate": 128
  }
}
```
Admission uses it for placement/credits; coalescer can use only latency_target_ms when shaping windows.

Ethan Cole: nice. we should also expose a boolean `prefetch_ok` for i/o-heavy prompts so admission can favor warm kvcache lanes.

Samir Gupta: semantics question: are budget hints soft or authoritative? i.e., do we treat them as hints the scheduler may violate under pressure? I prefer soft hints with monitoring and regression alerts.

Marta Lopez: +1 soft. if we ever enforce hard budgets we'd need billing hooks. soft hints allow graceful degradation policies: spill to dedicated pool, lower quant, or fallback model.

Ava Chen: about the interface boundary: admission -> returns AdmissionTicket {lease_id, credits, estimated_window_ms}. coalescer consumes AdmissionTicket but should never mutate scheduler state. ticket revocation via heartbeat only. avoids tight coupling.

Priya Nair: good. That implies a small ticket lifecycle: claim -> coalesce -> execute -> release. We need APIs for early release and forced revoke on overload.

Ethan Cole: observability: emit token-level traces with budget_hint and ticket lifecycle events. want to be able to reconstruct why a request got a tiny vs big window.

Samir Gupta: on long-term refactor: split admission into two phases: fast-path cheap heuristics (latency SLOs) and slow-path optimizer (packing for throughput). both share same metadata store but different time budgets. This helps keeping p99 low.

Marta Lopez: implementation note: keep admission idempotent for retries. multi-step admission must be safe under duplicate requests and client-side retries.

Ava Chen: experiments to run: 1) shared backlog + ephemeral handles with budget hints; 2) per-tenant queues with aggressive retirement; measure p50/p95/p99 and memory tail. run in canary with synthetic tenant fanout.

Priya Nair: action items: @Ethan create experiment harness (1 week), @Samir add budget_hint fields to request proto and wire up telemetry, @Marta prototype ephemeral handle lifecycle, @Ava draft ticket lifecycle API. I'll coordinate rollout gating and regression alerts.

deploy-bot: canary cluster deploy queued: batcher-v2-experiment -> staging. ETA 14m. :rocket:

Ethan Cole: noted, will start harness and share slack with metrics link.

Samir Gupta: starting proto PR today, will tag the team.

Marta Lopez: will prototype retire worker by Friday.

Ava Chen: drafting API now, will push RFC doc to /eng-runtime.

Priya Nair: thanks all — keeping this tight. if we find simple wins (budget_hint + ticket) we may avoid huge refactor. if not, split admission phases is our fallback.

title: #docs: 5467821111
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads-up 1 1 the release-notes/changelog draft is in https://git.redwood/docs/pull/904. Need: small migration blurb for embed-streaming deprecation, SDK snippet parity check (py + go + node), and a quick docs linkcheck pass. Can volunteers pick up pieces?
Jin Park: I'll own release notes copy and the final merge order. Please keep changes scoped (no endpoint URL edits in this PR).
Marta Gomez: I can do SDK snippets. Will update Python & Node samples to use the new streaming toggle and verify examples run. ETA: 5pm PT.
Kevin Liu: Running linkcheck now. Will open a separate PR with redirects/fixes.
DocsBot: nightly-linkcheck job complete  report: https://ci.redwood/linkcheck/546782; found 4 broken anchors, 2 external timeouts, 8 warnings.
Lea Ortiz: Migration blurb drafted. I called out the deprecation timeline and an example migration diff, but need someone to sanity-check the compatibility matrix numbers.
Samira Khan: I'll validate the perf and memory numbers in the matrix; found slight variance on large-context models.
Priya R.: Adding a Known-issues note: dedupe cache can increase first-token latency on cold starts for some quantized configs. Short blurb ready.
Noah: Small process ask  let's keep link fixes and snippet changes as separate PRs to make rollbacks easier. Last time we combined and it blocked the rollout.
Ava Chen: Agreed. I'll split the draft into three branches: notes-only, sdk-examples, linkfixes. Jin, please coordinate merge order so docs build stays green.
Kevin Liu: Linkfix PR opened: https://git.redwood/docs/pull/906  includes redirects and anchor rewrites. Left comments where manual redirects are required.
Marta Gomez: SDK PR: https://git.redwood/docs/pull/907. Node snippet needed an updated import path and a small await in the sample promise. Added short test harness code to keep snippets runnable.
Samira Khan: Perf numbers updated in the compatibility table and pushed. Left a note about rounding to avoid confusion between 1.9s vs 2.0s.
Lea Ortiz: Reviewed the migration wording and trimmed it to a single example diff + checklist. Pushed edits to the notes-only branch.
Priya R.: Known-issue blurb pushed to the notes branch; ask: tone ok? I kept it neutral and diagnostic.
Jin Park: Merge plan: linkfixes first (to unblock anchors), then SDK parity (examples only), then release notes + known issues. Target timeline: linkfixes by 3:30pm PT, SDK by 5pm PT, notes by 6pm PT. I'll do a final docs build check at 6:30pm and tag.
Ava Chen: Thanks all. If anything pops up, drop a short message here or ping @jin. I'll also add a short checklist to the PR description so reviewers know what's in-scope.
Noah: Thumbs up. I'll keep an eye on the staging site once linkfixes merge.

title: #eng-runtime: 4722223333
preview:
Ava Chen: Hey, noticed latency spike on seqlen=128 in canary after the new kernel-autotune changes. p50 jumped ~30ms and tail worse.
Rajat Singh: Ugh. Seen that too on the nvsm108 pool. Any error logs? perf-bot, did rollout notify?
perf-bot: [deploy] rollout/arch-autotune-2026-03-15 -> 5/5 pods updated :white_check_mark:
Maya Lopez: I pulled a flamegraph from the canary, looks like we lost our KV-cache fastpath for a subset of requests. Kernel selector fell back to the generic matmul path.
Jordan Kim: Do we have the device capability signature for those nodes? SM detection might be misreporting tensorCore BF16 support.
Ava Chen: Yep. Dump from runtime probe attached below.
```
DEVICE 0: sm=8.10, tc=available, bf16=available, fp8=unknown
runtime: selected kernel path=matmul_generic
kvcache: fastpath=false
```
Rajat Singh: fp8=unknown is suspicious. The selector will disable fp8 kernels if it can't confirm support. Could be the driver returning an incomplete caps report after the kernel change.
Maya Lopez: Quick repro: run batch=8, seqlen=128 on model x-small and watch kvcache evictions + chosen kernel. I can share a reproducer script.
Jordan Kim: Please run `./tools/probe-capabilities --device 0 --verbose` and paste output. Also collect `/var/log/runtime/kernelselect.log` lines around the request.
perf-bot: Perf regression alert created: PERF-4621
Ava Chen: Ran the probe, here's the log snippet:
```
[2026-03-15T12:03:01Z] probe: hw_sig=0x5f, sm=86 detected via pci
[2026-03-15T12:03:01Z] probe: tensorcores=true bf16=true fp8_probe_timeout
[2026-03-15T12:03:02Z] kernelselect: probe returned partial caps -> conservative route
```
Maya Lopez: Looks like a timeout during fp8 microprobe, maybe due to the new prefetch optimization starving the probe thread.
Rajat Singh: Temporary mitigation: add a config gating `probe.fp8_timeout_ms=3000` -> increase timeout so probe can finish, or short-term force-enable fp8 on nodes we trust.
Jordan Kim: We should be careful force-enabling. Patch idea: if probe returns partial caps but sm >= 8.6 and tc/bf16 true, allow fp8 trial with fallback to generic kernel on error.
Ava Chen: Agreed. I can put up a small canary: allow fp8 trial for 10% of traffic with aggressive fallback and KV cache warmup disabled. Rollback option ready.
Maya Lopez: Also need a perf test harness: seqlen 32/64/128/256 microbench with batching 1/4/8 to validate kernel picks and KV hit rates.
Rajat Singh: I'll add autotune metric tags to trace: selected_kernel, kv_fastpath, probe_status, device_sig. That will help query regressions.
perf-bot: Related issue linked PERF-4621 -> PR #7892
Jordan Kim: Plan: (1) repro & gather logs (Maya/Ava), (2) small canary fp8 trial (Ava), (3) autotune trace tags (Rajat), (4) merge if stable (everyone). Target: today EOD.
Ava Chen: On it. Creating canary now :rocket:
Maya Lopez: I'll run microbench and upload flamegraphs to the thread.
Rajat Singh: I'll prepare the rollback patch just in case. :thumbsup:
Jordan Kim: Thanks all — keep updates here.

title: #customer-success: 3987659876
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — Helios Labs wants a midterm adjustment: drop 12 seats and bill to their EU subsidiary. Procurement asks for delegated signer on DocuSign. Any compliance or finance blockers?

Noah Patel: Who's the current contract signer? Are they asking for credit or term change?

Priya Rao: Legal: delegation is OK if we get an official delegation email plus org chart. EU entity swap requires VAT/tax ID + remit address. No material T&C changes allowed. We need a short addendum referencing account id.

Liam O'Neill: Finance: need legal name, VAT, remit address. For seat drop we'll issue prorated credit. PO preferred but CC acceptable to speed up.

Ava Chen: Effective date requested 2026-06-15. They've sent a delegation pdf to procurement@redwood.com.

Marta (CS Manager): Can procurement email + doc suffice? @Priya confirm.

Priya Rao: Yes — procurement email from company domain + delegation doc acceptable. I'll draft one-page addendum now.

Noah Patel: I'll confirm delegated signer's name. If we can get CC we can close faster before PO.

Docusign Bot: Envelope 0xdef456: Sent to elena.t@helios.com for signature (1/3). Status: sent.

Ava Chen: Link to delegation doc: https://files.redwood/helios/delegation-0612.pdf

title: #eng-runtime: 3912345678
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — noticed a ~3x TTFT spike on /v1/chat around 10:20 UTC after the kernel-selector canary rollout. dashboard: https://console.redwood/team/rt/perf?panel=token_waterfall
Devon Patel: Saw it too. Decode time looks steady, but kernel_time attribution is much higher. Can you drop a token-level waterfall/probe?
Ava Chen: Quick probe dump pasted below:
```
route: /v1/chat  model: redwood-7b  seq_len: 128
avg_ttft: 240ms  (baseline ~80ms)
avg_decode_time: 60ms
avg_kernel_exec: 120ms
avg_kernel_queue: 40ms
kvcache_hit_rate: 0.82
kvcache_fetch_ms (p95): 90ms
selector_branch: microkernel-fallthrough (unexpected)
```
Ravi Singh: That selector_branch stands out — last selector patch added a fallthrough to microkernel for small seqs, but route seq distribution includes many 100+ prompts. Might be mis-evaluating effective seq_len due to tokenization differences.
Sasha Martín: Also seeing microbatch size distribution skew: many single-request batches after the scheduler tweak. Small batches raise per-token scheduling overhead.
observability-bot: automated probe summary: token_latency_breakdown (per-route /v1/chat): prequeue=30ms, kernel_queue=40ms, kernel_exec=120ms, postproc=50ms -> ttft=240ms
Devon Patel: kernel_exec 120ms is abnormally high. Question: is kernel_exec inflated because we include kvcache remote fetch time inside kernel time? We should separate kvcache_fetch_ms from kernel_exec in the waterfall.
Ava Chen: I traced one sample: kernel started, tried local shard, missed -> triggered remote shard fetch (89ms) then resumed kernel exec. So remote kvcache fetch is being counted inside kernel_exec in current instrumentation. snippet:
```
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kernel_start token_idx=12
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kvcache_get shard0 -> MISS
[2026-03-18T10:19:45Z] kvcache_fetch_remote(shard0) 89ms
[2026-03-18T10:19:46Z] kernel_exec resumed, exec_ms=30ms
```
Ravi Singh: Makes sense. Actionable items: (1) add explicit kvcache_fetch_ms field to token waterfall and metric labels, (2) add selector_branch tag to kernel metrics so we can attribute exec/queue by branch, (3) instrument microbatch_size in the probe to correlate small batches with TTFT.
Sasha Martín: I'll open a dashboard PR to add panels: kvcache_fetch_ms heatmap, selector_branch pie, microbatch_size histogram, and an alert: TTFT > 200ms sustained for 5m on /v1/chat.
Ava Chen: I'm going to rollback the selector canary for now and re-run the sampler. Pinging @infra-bot to start rollback. :pray:
infra-bot: rollback initiated for canary pool id=canary-3. Estimated completion: ~4m. :gear:
Devon Patel: After rollback completes, re-run token-sampler with the same probe config and post diffs (ttft/decode/kernel/kvcache). If TTFT returns to baseline we'll revert the change and do offline selector thresholds tuning.
Ava Chen: Agreed. I'll post results in ~10m. Thanks all. :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-runtime: 3419876543
preview:
Priya Patel: Heads-up — after the kv-prefetch tweak landed in canary, we're seeing 2-3x p95 for short sequences (<32 tokens) on nv8p. Latency cliff around 100ms -> 300ms for short-paths. I put a few request traces and flamegraphs in the perf gist. Anyone seen kernel/kvm interactions with prefetch?

Diego Morales: Yep saw the spike on dashboards. Micro-profiling shows an extra memcpy on the kernel selection hot path when prefetch engages. Batcher tends to coalesce a few short requests into a minislab which then picks a fused-kernel that has higher setup cost.

Ava Chen: Kernel chooser currently looks at seq-length histogram + kv_hit_estimate to pick fused vs fastpath. Prefetch warms KV and increments the "active tokens" accounting, which looks like longer sequences to the selector — could be double-counting KV warmup in the heuristic.

Marta Ivanov: Quick mitigation idea: add a "short-lane reserve" in the queue steward so short sequences get a small reserved token budget and avoid being coalesced with long ones. Also tune prefetch depth for short lanes. Draft config snippet below: 

```
queue_steward:
  short_lane:
    reserve_tokens_per_sec: 150
    max_coalesce_size: 4
    prefetch_depth: 1   # shallow prefetch for short-path
  long_lane:
    dynamic_fair_share: true
```

Priya Patel: Would that reserve reduce overall throughput for long tenants? Concern about unfairness / eviction overhead.

Diego Morales: We can make it proportional: short-lane gets a soft reserve (not hard cap) and we replenish from long-lane if idle. Also add a hedging factor so reserve scales by occupancy. On kernel side we can add a hint: if batch composition >= 80% short sequences prefer low-setup kernels.

Ava Chen: Agree. Add a kernel_hint label on batches (kernel_hint: prefer_low_setup) so selection avoids heavy fused kernels when prefetch/kv warmups are the dominant cost. Also record the reason in traces: admit_reason = "short_reserve" or "coalesced". That'll make A/B analysis easier.

infra-bot: deploy: canary/prefetch-harmony-v2 -> prod-rollout started by @ci (links: https://ci.redwood/rollouts/1234).

Priya Patel: Experiment plan: A/B 20% nodes for 24h, collect p50/p95/p99 by seq-length bucket (0-8,9-32,33-128) and kv_hit_rate. Add extra metrics: kernel_hint, admit_reason. I pushed a short smoke test: /infra/perf/smoke/short_prefetch_probe.sh

Diego Morales: I'll run the mini-bench and attach flamegraphs. For fast mitigation today we can set prefetch_depth=1 for short-path and reduce prefetch concurrency from 8->2. That should cut warmup memcpy contention.

Marta Ivanov: Ack. I'll prepare a rollback job and a small feature-flag flip so we can toggle short-lane reserve if the canary regresses. :thumbsup:

Priya Patel: Thanks all — prioritizing this for morning triage. Will post consolidated perf artifacts and a short checklist. :coffee:

title: #eng-ml: 3185432100
preview:
Ava Chen: Kicking off Nebula-13B v1 onboarding. Aim: pick a quant plan (int8 vs fp8 vs gguf) and run smoke + sensitivity. Primary workload: chat; secondary: codegen. Constraints: p95 latency <120ms on ded pool, quality delta vs fp16 <= 5%.
Ravi Patel: Sounds good. Priority is chat latency/quality. Embeddings can be deferred. Who owns the prompt suite?
Marta: I will. I'll assemble chat_regress_50, codegen_smoke_25, and redwood_eval_100. Light-weight prompts first to get quick signals.
calib-bot: Calibration job submitted: nebula13b:v1 -> int8(gguf) on pool-gpu-a. Job id c-8732. Estimated runtime 2h.
Jonas: Quick decision point: weight quantization per-channel symmetric or per-tensor? I recommend per-channel symmetric for stability; activations run both ABS-mean and KL.
Ava Chen: Per-channel for weights agreed. Activation probes: test KL, MSE, and bias-correction toggle. Add a smoke acceptance rule: perplexity delta <1.5% and rerank fidelity >=0.95.
Kai: For fp8 probe, any preference for shared exponent vs block-fp8? I can try block-FP8 profile tomorrow; will log matrix-wise overflow stats.
Ravi Patel: Add metrics we need: LM-eval subset scores, rerank fidelity, logit gap snapshots for failing prompts, and latency p50/p95. Auto-fail if rerank fidelity drops >5% on critical suite.
Marta: I'll add a short checklist in the model doc: 1) smoke with 10 prompts -> perf + quality, 2) seq-length sweep [128,512,2048], 3) sensitivity sweep on top-k/top-p behaviors, 4) hardware kernel perf matrix.
calib-bot: Smoke complete: int8(gguf) smoke results -> p95 latency 98ms, perplexity delta +0.9%, rerank fidelity 0.97. Tagging for full sensitivity sweep.
Jonas: Good start. I'll run the seq-length sweep and enable KV-cache heatmap logging. Expect preliminary artifacts in ~6h.
Ava Chen: If any failing cases, snapshot full logits + prompt context and pin the model version. Also record cost-per-token estimates for each quantization path.
Ravi Patel: Decision rule we agreed: if fp8 reaches comparable quality within 2% and reduces cost by >=20% vs int8, prefer fp8 for dedicated pools; otherwise default to int8 gguf.
Marta: Acknowledged. I'll synthesize results after sensitivity runs and post summary with clear pass/fail signals and recommended default quant profile. :party_parrot:

Diego Park: Quick heads-up — all-hands today at 11:00am PT. Jon Lee: will this change any of the Dedicated SLA or autoscale behavior? ops needs to know if we should update runbooks.

Diego Park: Dedicated unaffected. This is only hosted API quota controls + suggested defaults. But we will add an ops flag for early rollouts. Runbooks should get a note — tagging eng-sre and eng-platform to review.

Ivy (Legal): flagged — need to confirm the notice language for regulated customers before we auto-enforce. Can the legal team review the customer emails before send?

Diego Park: absolutely. Legal review before any enforcement emails. We'll schedule a 30-min sync after the all-hands.

title: #eng-oncall: 2890012345
preview:
incident-bot: PAGER: High error rate detected for Dedicated pool dp-123 in us-west-2. 5xx spike + queue length > 120s. Threshold breached at 2026-03-15T21:07:25Z. Paging on-call.
Ava Chen: ack I'm on it, looking at metrics now.
Ravi Patel: I just woke up, taking primary. Can someone paste the top offenders?
Elena Gomez: throwing up quick dashboard links: https://monitoring.redwood.io/dp-123/overview — latency and inflight look scary. Most requests are from api keys for ACME and BetaCorp.
Kai Matsumoto: logs show repeated KV cache misses + long tail (>20s) on tokenization step for many short requests.
Samir Khan: hypothesis: autoscaler didn't spawn burst workers due to a config mismatch on dp-123 (min_replicas=2, burst_enabled=false).
Ava Chen: if burst_enabled is false we should enable burst capacity now. Ravi do you want me to toggle?
Ravi Patel: yes please. Also start temporary rate limit on heavy customers while we flip burst.
Ava Chen: running command to enable burst capacity (will expire in 30m unless extended):
```bash
# enable burst for Dedicated pool dp-123
redwoodctl dedicated pools update --pool-id dp-123 --set burst_enabled=true --set burst_duration=30m
```
ops-bot: ACTION: update request received for pool dp-123 by Ava Chen. Job queued (job_id: jb-7723).
Ava Chen: job completed. burst_enabled=true, burst_nodes=8 added. Monitoring now.
Kai Matsumoto: seeing CPU nodes come up and KV hit rate improving. Latency dropping from 1.6s p95 -> 600ms p95. Nice.
Ravi Patel: starting targeted rate-limits for ACME and BetaCorp for next 15m:
```http
PATCH /admin/tenants/ACME/limits
Content-Type: application/json
{ "rate_limit": { "requests_per_minute": 300 } }
```
Elena Gomez: sent to prod-configs api, response 200.
Customer Success (Maya): Maya here — I messaged ACME account team: we told them we're doing a temporary throttling to stabilize infra, expect <10% degradation, will notify when lifted.
Samir Khan: also toggled streaming to best-effort for all Dedicated pools to reduce per-connection overhead. Command: `redwoodctl dedicated config set --pool dp-123 --streaming mode=best-effort`
Ava Chen: checking error rates now. 5xx counts down 82% in last 5m. Queue time <30s. Good.
Ravi Patel: marking mitigation successful for now. We still need root cause — why burst_enabled=false?
Nina Park: copying config history: dp-123 was created with template stable-v2 on 2025-11-02; a recent infra migration on 2026-02-28 accidentally set burst_enabled=false on pools using stable-v2. Patch in infra repo open: gh/infra#842.
Ava Chen: ok note that in incident thread. Also need to confirm we didn't exceed dedicated commit limits while adding burst nodes. Finance alert?
Samir Khan: burst was within allowance for emergency bursts per contract; ops-bot confirmed no billing alert triggered.
Ravi Patel: prepare for handoff — I'm going to bed in 20m. Ava can you own next 2-hour watch and run a short postmortem draft?
Ava Chen: yes. I'll keep monitoring for 30m, then hand off to Elena at 00:00 UTC.
Elena Gomez: acknowledged. I'll compile the timeline and add ACME impact estimate.
Ava Chen: customer impact: ACME saw errors for ~12 minutes, approx 1.8k failed requests total. BetaCorp minor (<200 fails); other customers unaffected.
Customer Success (Maya): I've queued a proactive message to both customers offering status and ETA.
Ravi Patel: adding follow-ups: 1) infra migration rollback rule to preserve burst flag 2) add alert to detect pool with high queue + burst_disabled 3) run chaos test to ensure autoscaler spawns nodes under spike.
Nina Park: created follow-up tickets: INFRA-1123, ALERTS-778, CHAOS-55.
Ava Chen: handing off to Elena in 10m. Leaving note: burst was a valid emergency knob, but we should avoid manual forever toggles.
Elena Gomez: taking over. Will escalate to Platform PM for config template change.
Ravi Patel: final status before sleep: mitigated via burst enable + targeted rate-limits + streaming downgrade. Recommend 72hr RCA with logs and exact timing. Good job team. :thumbsup:
incident-bot: INCIDENT: dp-123 high-error resolved. Duration 00:28:55. Severity: Sev2. Postmortem link draft: https://docs.redwood.internal/incidents/dp-123-burst-mitigation-2890012345 (draft)
Ava Chen: will fill postmortem and include exact CLI commands & metrics graphs.
Elena Gomez: will own customer communications follow-through.
Nina Park: logging handoff in oncall rotation: next oncall - Kai.
Kai Matsumoto: acknowledged, will keep an eye overnight.
Ava Chen: thanks all.
Ravi Patel: good night.

title: #customer-success: 2765432100
preview:
Ava Chen: FYI I pushed a small script to sync the onboarding asset checklist across playbooks. Noticed a handful of accounts missing the `welcome_call` step in HubSpot -> playbook mapping, which blocks our automated enablement emails. Rolling it out as a dry-run in staging tonight. Ping me if this would interfere with any ongoing backfills :wave:

Tom Ruiz: Thanks Ava — where's the repo/PR? want to skim before staging run. Also curious if this touches healthscore fields or just playbook metadata.

Ava Chen: PR here: https://github.com/redwood-inference/cs-ops/PR/482 (branch: cs/onboard-check-sync). It's additive — creates `onboard_assets_missing` boolean and appends missing steps to the playbook metadata. Does NOT change the computed healthscore.

Maya Patel: Phew ok. If it's only metadata we can still have playbooks pick it up for the next run. Can we do a backfill for all accounts created in the last 90 days after the staging verification? Some churned trials are impacted.

Ava Chen: Yep plan is: 1) run on staging with `--dry-run` to produce a CSV; 2) review sample of 100 accounts; 3) if looks good, run a one-off backfill in prod over 90d window. Backfill command: `python tools/sync_onboard_assets.py --window 90 --apply`

Jonah Klein: Can you paste the SQL you used to detect missing steps? want to ensure no joins that explode.

Ava Chen: SQL used to find gaps — lightweight:
```sql
SELECT a.account_id, p.playbook_id, s.step_key
FROM accounts a
LEFT JOIN playbook_steps p ON p.playbook_id = a.playbook_id
LEFT JOIN account_steps s ON s.account_id = a.account_id AND s.step_key = p.step_key
WHERE p.required = true AND s.step_key IS NULL
LIMIT 100;
```
Should be fine for incremental runs; we batch per playbook to keep queries small.

Tom Ruiz: Cool. Two asks: 1) add a `dry_run_sample` flag so we can easily rerun with a deterministic seed; 2) emit a small audit event for each change so CS can review in the console later.

Ava Chen: Good calls — added `--seed` and I emit `cs.onboard.asset_sync` events to `cs-audit` topic. Events include account_id, before_steps, after_steps, actor.

Maya Patel: About risk scoring — can we have an opt-in toggle so accounts flagged as "missing_welcome_call" don't change renewal risk until cs-review? We don't want false positives in churn alerts.

Ava Chen: Yes, default is opt-out (no risk changes). I added an optional `apply_risk_tag=true` flag for the backfill, but it's off by default. We'll coordinate with risk team before turning it on.

Jonah Klein: Sounds good. I'll schedule a staging dry-run tomorrow 09:30 PT and invite the risk / enablement folks to review the sample CSV. Please push any small fixes today so I can include them.

CS Ops Bot: deploybot: Rolling deployment for `cs/onboard-check-sync` to staging started by Ava Chen — build #842 queued. :rocket:

Ava Chen: Thanks — I'll post the dry-run CSV to #customer-success when complete and attach the sampled accounts. If anyone wants a pre-review of the CSV format DM me. :memo:

title: #all-hands: 2345678901
preview:
Sam Nguyen: Thanks for joining the follow-up thread from today's all-hands. I want a concise record of the runtime/canary decisions we made.
Ava Chen: I'll paste the leadership answers so folks can act. Short and actionable.
Priya Rao: first question: what's our formal definition of 'regression' for kernel canaries?
Ava Chen: regression = any of: (1) canary p99 latency > baseline p99 * 1.12 for 10m, (2) error rate > 0.4% sustained for 10m, (3) eval prompt F1 drop >= 2% on core prompt set. Any hit triggers auto-rollback and an alert.
Kai Patel: are those baselines per-model or global?
Ava Chen: per-model baseline. Baselines are the last stable release window median; stored in telemetry. ENG-5040 will expose an API to query baselines.
Marta Lopez: follow-up about targets: what are our service targets we should show in docs and to customers?
Sam Nguyen: agreed we need public-facing SLOs. For Dedicated: short-turn p95 <= 120ms, p99 <= 300ms. Long-context (8k+) p95 <= 600ms, p99 <= 1.25s. For Hosted free-tier we'll say "best-effort" with typical medians in benchmarks.
Diego Rivera: for batching: any short guidance for devs that onboard models? We saw head-of-line blocking in the last rollout.
Ava Chen: recommended defaults: 1) route short queries (<256 tokens) to short route with microbatching target 64ms latency cap, 2) long queries to separate pool with batching optimized for throughput. If mixed traffic is unavoidable, enable sequence-length aware batching or set dynamic max-batch-size to avoid H-O-L.
Priya Rao: can we get a sample canary command in the playbook? Micro-canary recipe would help SEs.
Ava Chen: adding example now below playbook and in this thread:
```
# micro-canary: 10-minute run at 20% of target QPS
canary-run --model=kernel-v3-opt --qps=50 --duration=600 --ratio=0.2 --tags=team-canary
# post-run: check dashboards/canaries/<run-id> and run evals
```
Kai Patel: dashboard pieces: I'll put p50/p95/p99, error rate, and request size histogram. Also adding an 'anomaly' band for canary baseline comparison.
Marta Lopez: I'll own the playbook PR. Plan: PR by Thu EOD with sections: thresholds, rollback, play-by-play canary steps, postmortem checklist.
Sam Nguyen: ownership noted. What about communication to customers when kernels change that could affect outputs?
Ava Chen: release policy: For behavioral/determinism-impacting kernel changes, we post release notes + alert banner for Dedicated customers 72 hours before rollout. For Private deployments, contacts will be notified and given migration notes.

title: #eng-runtime: 2248903333
preview:
Ava Chen: OOM spike observed on gpu-pool-3 just now. allocator failed to reserve workspace for batch=8, seq_len=2048 during a KV warmup surge. stack excerpt attached below.

Joel Park: I saw the same. kernel selector jumped to a full-fprop matmul path and memory jumped ~1.8GB. batching heuristic allowed batch growth during warmup which made it worse.

Sana R.: Can we do a quick failover: immediate circuit-breaker -> route to quantized-variant + reduce max_batch from 8 to 4 for that pool? That should avoid large workspace allocations.

Joel Park: +1. Also propose a short-term allocator tweak: prefer workspace reuse over fresh reserve when free_mem_pct < 25%. simple config: 
```
allocator: {
  prefer_reuse_threshold_pct: 25,
  max_new_reserve_mb: 512
}
```
This drops 90% of the transient OOMs in my local repro.

Mike L.: Add metric/alert: `allocator.reserve_fail_rate` and `kvcache.miss_burst` > 50 over 1m. That would trigger circuit-breaker before user-impact.

deploy-bot: route update: `pool-3` -> `quant-v2` canary applied (60s ago). rolling rule reduces `max_batch` to 4 for matches with seq_len > 1024.

Ava Chen: Nice. Can we also add a temporary request-shaping rule: drop/trim requests with seq_len > 4096 and send 413 with suggestion header? Better to fail fast than cascade OOM.

Sana R.: I'll add the request-shaper config. snippet: 
```
request_shaper: {
  max_seq_len: 4096,
  fallback_policy: { type: "reject_with_hint", hint: "try_quantized_or_smaller_seq" }
}
```

Joel Park: I'll open PR for the allocator tweak + dynamic_batch_cap heuristic (cap = floor(free_mem_mb / per_token_ws_mb / 2)). Will include unit tests and perf microbenchmark.

Mike L.: I'll wire up the dashboard panels for `allocator.reserve_fail_rate`, `free_mem_percentile_10m`, and `kvcache.eviction_rate`. Also create alert playbook linking this thread.

Ava Chen: Thanks. Quick rollback plan: if canary increases tail latency > 100ms or error rate > 0.5% in 5m, rollback route to previous variant and restore max_batch=8.

Sana R.: Scheduling canary for 20:00 PST today and will monitor.

Joel Park: PR coming in ~30m.

Ava Chen: Action items: Joel -> PR for allocator + batching heuristic; Sana -> request-shaper + canary ops; Mike -> metrics + alert; ops -> update runbook with circuit-breaker steps. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 2178004321
preview:
Ava Chen: heads up — around 09:42 UTC we saw p99 jump from ~220ms to ~1.3s on a handful of requests. correlates with a KV cache eviction spike when concurrency hit ~620 on g4-prod pools. not a full outage but SLOs are unhappy. anyone on call?

Miguel Rojas: looking. do we have a flamegraph / profile from the time window? also which kernel was active when the tail spiked?

Riya Patel: sharing quick profile + logs. flamegraph (sample): https://perf.redwood/internal/flame/2178004321.html — shows heavy time in `kv_lookup` + `kv_evict` and then long stalls in `kernel_dispatch` due to fallback to generic matmul path.

Riya Patel: short log excerpt:
```
[09:42:13] kv: miss -> evict key=usr-embedding-xxxx
[09:42:13] kv: evict start (heap=0.83)
[09:42:13] runtime: switch kernel=opt_gemm -> fallback_generic (seq=512)
[09:42:13] request: wait for kv-lock 12ms
[09:42:14] request: kernel fallback + microbatch stall 480ms
```

perf-bot: :warning: perf-alert: p99_latency > 1s for route `/v1/generate` (prod-us-west) — correlated: KV eviction events increased by 4.1x in 1m window.

Kenji Sato: quick hypothesis: evictions causing KV miss storm which inflates seq lengths hitting kernels that don't have optimized kernels (or wrong kernel chosen due to cache miss timing). microbatch gating currently uses fixed `max_mb_per_worker=12`. at high concurrency that leads to more per-request cache churn.

Ava Chen: proposals? fast mitigations first please.

Riya Patel: immediate mitigation we can try (non-disruptive): raise `kv_cache_hysteresis` from 0.6 -> 0.82 and lower `max_mb_per_worker` to 8 for prod-us-west via hot-config. command: `redwoodctl set runtime.kv_cache.hysteresis 0.82 --env=prod-us-west` and `redwoodctl set runtime.batch.max_mb_per_worker 8 --env=prod-us-west`

Miguel Rojas: agree. also add short-term kernel selection tweak: prefer `opt_int8_gemm` for sequences > 256 when `kv_miss_rate > 0.15` to avoid fallback penalty. config snippet:
```yaml
runtime:
  kernel_selection:
    fallback_policy: prefer_quant_when_kv_unstable: true
    kv_miss_threshold: 0.15
```

Kenji Sato: for safety I'd canary the batch change across 10% of nodes first. we should also add a micro probe that measures p99 conditioned on kv_hit_rate buckets.

Ava Chen: can we get a quick before/after test locally? Riya can you run a replay over a 10k req window with concurrency ramp to 700?

Riya Patel: ran a replay on staging-2 with config tweaks. results (p95/p99):
```
base (no changes): p95=210ms p99=980ms
with hysteresis 0.82 + max_mb=8: p95=230ms p99=320ms
+ kernel_tweak prefer_quant: p95=225ms p99=300ms
```

Miguel Rojas: nice — major win on tail. also looked at CPU side: eviction IO thrash dropped by ~60% in the replay.

Ava Chen: ok let's do the 10% canary rollout of the batch change + kernel tweak. Riya/Kenji to own rollout, Miguel to monitor perf dashboards and revert if p99 > 600ms. I'll notify infra and support.

Kenji Sato: scheduling canary now — will post canary thread. rolling to 10% in 5m.

perf-bot: :white_check_mark: canary started (10% nodes) — watch keys: /dashboard/p95, /dashboard/p99, /alerts/p95regress

Ava Chen: thanks everyone. follow-up action items: (1) add kv-hit-rate conditioned p99 metric to per-route dashboards (Miguel), (2) explore adaptive microbatcher that lowers batch size when kv_eviction_rate rises (we'll scope next sprint, Kenji/Riya).

Riya Patel: noted. I'll document the hot-config commands and link flamegraph to the incident doc.

Miguel Rojas: I'm leaving a quick profiling run command here for the runbook: `pprof --text /tmp/prod-2178004321.pb | head -n 50`

Ava Chen: :thumbsup: good work. tagging this thread for postmortem TL;DR.

title: #eng-runtime: 2175000123
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — I kicked off the new bench-harness nightly run across Ampere/H100 for the candidate kernel set, full seqlen sweep 1..8192, microbatch sizes 1/2/4.
perf-bot: Run started: id=nn-2026-03-17-01, jobs=48, config=arch-aware+kv-probe
Diego Morales: Noted. Which candidate showed issues?
Ava Chen: candidate: kgen-v2-archmix-17. At seqlen=4096 we see p99 latency spike and throughput drop ~27% on H100. On Ampere it's fine. Uploading flamegraph and perf data.
Ava Chen: ```bash
# repro command
./bench --kernel kgen-v2-archmix-17 --seq 4096 --batch 1 --hw h100 --warm 50 --iter 200
```
Priya Nair: did you check KV cache path? That's where seqlen-specific code paths tend to diverge.
Ava Chen: yes, toggled KV on/off. With KV off the spike disappears. With KV on, perf trace shows extra memcpy in gather step (memcpy3 at gather_kv.c:214).
Samir Gupta: Could be alignment/packing on H100 kernels. kgen-v2 uses a vectorized gather that falls back to slow path for sequences that align a certain way. Strange pattern though.
Diego Morales: Also check batching heuristic: maybe continuous batching merges prefixes differently at seqlen boundary causing inefficient kernel selection. Which kernel was actually picked by runtime at seq=4096?
Ava Chen: Runtime picked kernel id=archmix_gather_kv_fast then fell back to archmix_gather_scalar after ~120ms. Autotuner metric shows "failed_perf=1.28x" and logged: selected=archmix_gather_kv_fast,fallback=archmix_gather_scalar
Priya Nair: Maybe autotuner mismeasured because of microbench noise. Re-run microprobe with isolation (CPU affined, no other jobs).
Ava Chen: Requeued an isolated microprobe run: mp-2175000128. Will post results.
perf-bot: mp-2175000128 completed: archmix_gather_kv_fast throughput=435 t/s, archmix_gather_scalar throughput=600 t/s at seq=4096 on H100. Noise sigma ~2.1%.
Samir Gupta: That's a big delta. Sounds like archmix_gather_kv_fast regresses on H100 for that seqlen. We should mark it flaky and block from nightly promotions.
Diego Morales: Agreed. Proposal: gating rule — if variant <0.85 of best for seq in critical set {1024,2048,4096} on a given architecture, fail candidate.
Priya Nair: Also add a KV-specific microbenchmark to the harness that exercises gather+kv heavy path across seqlen boundaries (including multiples of 256/512).
Ava Chen: I'll open PR to update bench-harness: add kv-heavy probe, tighten noise thresholds, ensure isolated nodes for H100 tests. Tentative PR: #3124.
Liam O'Connor: Also note mitigation: quick fix is to fallback to scalar gather on H100 until vectorized path is fixed. Prepare rollout plan (canary -> full).
Ava Chen: Plan: 1) block promotion for kgen-v2-archmix-17 2) runtime config: enforce fallback for H100 for seq=4096 3) debug/fix kernel 4) re-run full sweep.
Diego Morales: Any chance this is driver-related? What driver/CUDA versions on failing runs?
Ava Chen: H100 host: driver 525.85, CUDA 12.1. Ampere runs used CUDA 11.8. I'll repro with driver matrix.
Samir Gupta: If driver dependent, schedule driver-matrix sweep (525.x vs 530.x, CUDA 12.1/12.2).
perf-bot: Nightly sweep summary: candidates_evaluated=42, blocked=1 (kgen-v2-archmix-17), flaky=2, regressions_auto-blocked=1.
Priya Nair: Thanks. Ava, once PR is up and you have repro notes ping #eng-runtime and cc me.
Ava Chen: Will do. Uploading logs: https://files.redwood/test-runs/mp-2175000128.tar.gz :thumbsup:
Diego Morales: Good triage. Let's also add a short checklist in the harness README for seqlen-sensitive kernels (KV toggles, isolated runs, driver matrix).
Ava Chen: Adding checklist to PR. Closing loop when mp re-run + driver sweep finish.

preview:
Ava Chen: I need to swap primary tonight 21:00->07:00 PT. I'm on a delayed flight. Can someone confirm they can take primary for that window?
Samir Patel: I can take primary 21:00-03:00 PT, but I have a meeting at 01:00 PT. Happy to be primary until 03:00 and then hand to Diego if he's able.
Diego Martinez: I can pick up 03:00-07:00 PT. Will be available to respond to pages.
Ava Chen: Thanks both. Confirming plan: Samir primary 21:00-03:00, Diego primary 03:00-07:00. Maya you're secondary for whole window?
Maya Li: Yes, I'll be secondary 21:00-07:00 PT. I'll keep the incident dashboard open and monitor error budget.
Ava Chen: Quick reminders for handover: 1) Check incident-bot for active pages. 2) Run this quick smoke check against the production inference gateway: ```curl -s -X POST https://api.redwood-inference.com/v1/healthz -H 'Authorization: Bearer $ONCALL_TOKEN' | jq .``` 3) If you see latency > 400ms p50, follow the 'latency-spike' runbook (links below).
Samir Patel: Copy. Where is the runbook?
Ava Chen: Runbook link: https://redwood.internal/runbooks/latency-spike and the paging policy: https://redwood.internal/oncall/paging-policy. Also note: embeddings shard 3 sometimes flakes under burst. If you get continuous 5xx from /v1/embeddings that's likely shard-3.
incident-bot: :rotating_light: Pager triggered: P1 - prod-embeddings QPS surge (pagerduty #INC-8243). Affected customers: yes (upsell tier). Triggered at 20:42 PT. Current status: investigating.
Samir Patel: Seen the incident-bot alert. I'm already on the call with infra. ETA to mitigation ~12 mins. I'll assume primary early and keep everyone posted.
Diego Martinez: I'll be on standby and join the call if escalation continues past 21:30 PT.
Maya Li: If Samir needs relief mid-incident, Diego can claim primary by typing `/oncall takeover 2151234567` (devex helper). Also make sure to post a short public note in #incidents when switching.
Samir Patel: On the call: root cause looks like a mis-routed KV cache invalidation that caused hot recompile on one of the embedding workers. We're throttling new requests to that shard and routing 20% to fallback quantized model.
Ava Chen: Good mitigation plan. Samir, when you hand off at 03:00 PT: 1) post in this thread that you're handing off, 2) confirm no active P1s, 3) run `kubectl get pods -n inference -l shard=3` and paste pod statuses.
Samir Patel: Will do. If the P1 is still active at 03:00 I'll extend to 04:00 and ping Diego.
Diego Martinez: Sounds good. One ask for handover: Samir, please dump current top traces to S3 with the command in the runbook so I can pick up right away.
Samir Patel: Yep running the trace dump now: ```aws s3 cp /tmp/trace-2151234567.json s3://redwood-oncall-dumps/trace-2151234567.json``` 
Maya Li: Logging the coverage: primary rota updated in PagerDuty notes and calendar invite updated. I'll also add a quick checklist in the handoff doc: https://redwood.internal/oncall/handoffs/night-standby
Ava Chen: Thanks team. Final check: anyone unable to respond to a page during their window? If not, I'll mark the swap in the schedule. :thumbsup:
Diego Martinez: all good from me.
Samir Patel: good to go.
Ava Chen: Swap confirmed. I'll update the schedule and set an auto-ack reminder for 20:55 PT before you go primary, Samir. Good luck and ping if anything changes. :raised_hands:

title: #product: 2149999900
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick design review request 1: Autoscale onboarding: SLO slider + min/max + burst. Need microcopy + a11y sweep.
Liam Patel: Looked through. Biggest issue: SLO slider default set to 92% which forces high min. New users get surprised. Propose softer default.
Sofi Gomez: Accessibility flag: pool picker focus gets stuck after open/close on VoiceOver. Also duplicate ARIA labels on the pool cards. Tab order currently non-linear.
Marco Rivera: Users keep asking whether "burst" is permanent. Suggest explicit label or short hint: "Burst (temporary)". Also add a tooltip about billing.
Ellie Tran: Fallback messaging is too vague. When fallback to hosted happens we should say whats routed where and any perf/privacy differences. Current toast "Fallback active" -> not enough.
Ava Chen: Proposal: set SLO slider soft default to 70% with helper: "Start conservative; adjust after a few hours". Also show inline cost delta when they slide. Thoughts?
Liam Patel: 70% seems reasonable. Cost delta next to slider is a must for finance-conscious teams. Tiny text with estimated $/hour change.
Sofi Gomez: For screen readers: slider needs aria-valuetext like "Reliability 70 percent; higher reliability may increase cost". Pool picker should be role=dialog with aria-modal and a descriptive id referenced by aria-describedby.
Marco Rivera: Draft burst helper copy options: Option A: "Temporary burst GPUs for spikes; auto-scale down after 10 minutes." Option B (short): "Short-term extra GPUs (auto-remove)."
Ellie Tran: Draft fallback toast (v1):
```
Fallback active: Selected GPU pool full. Redirecting requests to Redwood Hosted for availability. Data remains encrypted in transit. To avoid routing, reserve Dedicated capacity. (Details)
```
Ellie Tran: Also recommend a non-modal inline banner in the UI when a fallback is active linking to a detailed explanation of performance and privacy tradeoffs.
Ava Chen: UX idea: if min=0 and burst enabled, show a small sparkline / micro-forecast under the min/max row illustrating tail latency and cold starts. Helps set expectations.
Liam Patel: Will prototype the cost delta UI and a tiny sparkline. Will try to keep it subtle so it doesnt overwhelm the control.
Sofi Gomez: Ill run keyboard + VoiceOver pass tomorrow and drop notes in Figma with specific aria snippets.
Marco Rivera: Ill send final microcopy (burst + tooltip + slider hint) with two tone options by EOD.
deploy-bot: deployment note: autoscale-ui-v2 deployed to staging at 2024-11-06T16:22Z. Please validate keyboard/a11y flows before promoting.
Ellie Tran: Next steps: Marco + Ava update copy; Liam prototypes cost delta + sparkline; Sofi a11y pass; Ill write fallback modal content. Target: EOD Thursday for staging verification.
Ava Chen: Thanks all 44f 3fb 680 - I'll update the figma and post the link here once done.

title: #sales: 2141598765
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick kickoff note — I put together a compact presales playbook for the Acme pilot. Need alignment on the eval 'gradebook' (golden set size, scoring weights, regression gates), security questionnaire path, and any pricing constraints that would block the PoC. TL;DR: 2-week test window, can we confirm?

Raj Patel: 2-week window sounds fine. Pricing Q: if they want Dedicated for predictable latency, do we quote committed monthly + overage, or a flat pilot price? Need guidance for commercial.

Tess Morgan: For pilots we usually offer a short-term reserved bundle (4 weeks of reduced commit) + hourly overage. But for enterprise buyers we can do a fixed pilot fee that covers reserved capacity and support. I can draft numbers once we lock expected qps and model size.

Miguel Santos: Ops question — what throughput/latency SLO are we using as a success metric? Also need the dataset footprint to size dedicated GPUs.

Tess Morgan: Proposed success criteria (suggested):
- Latency: p95 generation <= 250ms for typical prompts (seq len ~512)
- Quality: mean human score >= 4.0/5 on golden set (see scoring below)
- Recall / factualness: automatic recall@5 >= 0.88 on retrieval tasks
- Cost guardrail: pilot average token cost <= $0.012/token (configurable)

Jordan Lee: For gradebook we recommend: 150 golden examples (50 intent tests, 50 extraction/factual, 50 multi-turn flows). Hold out 60 examples for regression gates. Seed set for prompting/temperature tuning = 300 examples.

Ava Chen: That distribution sounds good. We need a simple scoring rubric so product owners can grade quickly. Jordan, can you paste a compact example?

Jordan Lee: Sure — example scoring rubric (weights):
```
{ "intent_accuracy": 0.35, "extraction_f1": 0.35, "fluency": 0.15, "safety": 0.15 }
```
Manuals: intent -> binary correct/incorrect, extraction -> F1 per field, fluency -> 1-5 Likert, safety -> 1-5 Likert (policy hits = 0). Aggregate to weighted mean.

Raj Patel: For commercial ask — if they request model pinning + audit logs (enterprise), we add the Private deployment add-on fee. Will surface numbers after Tess drafts the pilot fee.

Miguel Santos: Dataset/infra notes: if golden set ~150 examples (avg 800 tokens), seed 300, regressions 60, we need ~5–10GB of storage for eval artifacts and KV caches for repeated runs. Dedicated: 1 A100 equivalent should be enough for 100 qps at this config.

Ava Chen: On regression gates: propose simple rule — if weighted mean drops by >5% vs baseline on holdout, fail and rollback. Also block release if any safety metric drops by >1 point. Thoughts?

Jordan Lee: Agree. Also add automation: run reg tests nightly during pilot. If regression triggered, open an incident in #release-war-room and send alerts to the deal team.

Tess Morgan: Prompt-set design: aim for concise templates + templated metadata per example: {scenario, prompt_template, expected_entities, accept_variants}. Example prompt snippet below: 
```
Prompt: "Summarize the customer's request and list 3 action items."
Expected: summary (1-2 sentences), actions: list of 3 bullets
```
We should capture 2 golden prompts per flow to cover variance.

Ava Chen: Security questionnaire: LegalBot uploaded a filled SQA draft (answers + outstanding asks). Link: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/sqa-draft (internal)

LegalBot: New SQA draft uploaded by Legal: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/sqa-draft — outstanding: data residency clarification, encryption-at-rest key owner, and SOC attestation docs.

Raj Patel: Those are typical asks. We can provide KMS integration details and our SOC snapshot. Need Legal to confirm export controls.

Miguel Santos: Blocker note: customer asked for on-prem evaluation possibility. That adds 3 weeks to timeline for packaging + security review. Flagging to the deal team.

Ava Chen: Updated timeline proposal:
- Week 0: finalize gradebook + SQA answers + commercial terms
- Week 1: ingest golden set + seed set, run baseline evaluation, smoke test infra
- Week 2: closed-loop refinement (prompt tuning), nightly regression runs, demo to stakeholders at end of week
If on-prem is required, shift to 5-week plan.

Jordan Lee: Action items (assign):
- Jordan: prepare gradebook templates + tooling export (csv + json) — due EOD tomorrow
- Tess: draft pilot fee + Dedicated vs pilot flat fee options — due +48h
- Miguel: confirm infra sizing + KMS integration steps — due +48h
- Raj: confirm commercial caveats around on-prem and add-on language — due +72h
- LegalBot / Legal: finalize SQA answers and SOC docs — due +72h

Ava Chen: I'll consolidate into a one-page playbook for the exec sponsor after we get these items. Thanks team. :thumbsup:

Tess Morgan: ++ — will ping finance for the draft numbers.

Miguel Santos: Noted. I'll also add a quick runbook for nightly regression automation.

Raj Patel: I'll prep contract language for pilot terms and on-prem clause.

Jordan Lee: uploading gradebook template now: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/gradebook.csv

Ava Chen: Perfect — closing thread for now, will reopen if new blockers. :sparkles:

preview:
OncallBot: :rotating_light: INCIDENT INC-3821 (P1) — spike in /v1/generate 5xxs detected. Pager triggered to @eng-oncall. Link: https://go.redwood/incidents/INC-3821

Ava Chen: taking the 09:00-17:00 shift now. I see INC-3821 is active — Priya, are you still driving this?

Priya Nair: yes I'm on it, been triaging for ~18m. Root cause looks like OOM on one of the dedicated pools during a burst of quantized llama requests. Working hypothesis: an autoscaler race + a model variant with higher memory.

Ava Chen: ok — I'm taking comms if that's ok. Priya, can you keep driving mitigation and call out if you need a hand? I'm pulling the runbook and will post links. :wave:

Priya Nair: fine with that. quick notes: cluster rtx-prod-us-west-2 node group gpud-2 has two pods in CrashLoopBackOff. Logs show KV cache allocation failing.

Diego Rivera: heads up, I'm on the road but reachable by phone. I can be secondary for driver if needed. Not on VPN right now tho. :phone:

Ava Chen: noted — so ownership: Priya driving, Diego secondary standby, Ava comms. If Priya needs to escalate to Dedicated team we loop in @eng-runtime.

Sam Ortiz: I'm available to draft the incident summary for customers and PS if we confirm customer-facing impact. What is current customer impact?

Priya Nair: impact: ~12% of requests to /v1/generate returned 5xx in last 12m; error rate concentrated on requests hitting model redwood/llama-quant-8bit@v2 with sequence length > 1024. No broad auth failures.

Ava Chen: thanks. probable customer impact: degraded generation for some large-seq workloads, potential latency spikes. Sam, please prepare a short note — "We’re investigating elevated 5xx errors for large-generation requests; mitigation underway". I'll post to #customer-status once we have mitigation steps.

Priya Nair: mitigation steps I'm running now (from runbook https://go.redwood/runbooks/p1-api-errors):
```
# temporarily pin traffic off the problematic model variant
redwood route update /v1/generate --model redwood/llama-quant-8bit@v2 --weight 0.0
# scale up autoscaler min for gpud-2
kubectl -n inference scale deployment/gpud-2-autoscaler --replicas=4
# apply memory guard on model container
kubectl -n inference set env deploy/llama-quant --env="MODEL_MEM_GUARD=1"
```
I'll run the route update first and watch error rate.

Diego Rivera: if route update drops success rate elsewhere, be ready to rollback. Also check cache eviction metrics — prefix caching may be causing memory pressure.

Ava Chen: Priya, when you execute the route update, post the command output and short status summary here. Sam, draft posted customer note and I'll sign off. Diego, stand by for escalation.

Priya Nair: ran route update — returned 200, traffic rebalanced. Monitoring: error rate down to 1.9% (from 12%). Logs show fewer OOM traces. Still seeing sample pod crashes but not increasing.

Sam Ortiz: draft for #customer-status: "We are investigating elevated 5xx errors affecting a subset of large-generation requests. Mitigation in progress; we have temporarily routed traffic away from a model variant and are monitoring. We will provide an update in 30 minutes or sooner." Want me to post? :eyes:

Ava Chen: post that, please. Add link to incident page. :thumbsup:

OncallBot: Incident INC-3821 status updated to mitigation in progress by Priya Nair. Assigned roles: driver=Priya Nair, comms=Ava Chen, secondary=Diego Rivera.

Priya Nair: continuing to watch. next steps: let autoscaler settle for 5m, check pod restart frequency, if crashes continue I'll open a debug shell on the failing node and capture core dump.

Ava Chen: ok team — I'll take notes for the handoff at 13:00 if this is still open. If everything stabilizes, we'll close the incident and sync a postmortem owner. Thanks everyone. :heart:

title: #eng-ml: 2141234567
preview:
Ava Chen: quick brain dump — I'm prototyping a hotstart heuristic for safetensors sharded models. aim = cut p99 cold-start by ~2-3x for interactive routes. idea: score shards by (estimated token-impact, in-memory size, recent-access probability) and prefetch top-K. open to tradeoffs.
Ravi Patel: nice. can you share the scoring formula you're thinking? also worth calling out manifest vs index-first tradeoffs for discovery.
Ava Chen: sketch:
```
priority = alpha * token_impact + beta * access_freq - gamma * size_bytes
where token_impact ~ sum(prob(token in early positions) * param_count_per_shard)
K is dynamic based on free_gpu_mem fraction
```
Ravi Patel: hmm token_impact might be noisy for new prompts. maybe use a warmup bucket for embeddings+first transformer block always higher weight. also fallback policy: if index not found, try manifest -> fallback to sequential shard list.
Jin Park: +1 to pin first N small-but-hot shards (embeddings, layer_norms). we saw in perf tests that grabbing 1-2 small shards reduces latency more than one big shard.
Marta Gomez: watch mem spikes. prefetching many shards concurrently caused OOM on rtx-pool in my local run. we should add backpressure and an in-flight limit per worker, e.g. inflight_prefetch_limit=3.
Ava Chen: noted. propose inflight limit + exponential backoff on evictions. also consider streaming KV cache priming so we don't block tokens waiting for full shard.
infra-bot: system: worker-group/rtx-pool available_mem=5.2GB, inflight_prefetch=0
Ravi Patel: another tradeoff: index-first means one small index lookup RPC then targeted object reads — good for networked stores. manifest-first (full manifest blob) reduces RPC count at cost of bigger initial transfer. for cloud object stores, index-first wins; for local disk or tarball packaging, manifest-first may be faster.
Jin Park: can we support both with a unified loading API? e.g. load_shard(shard_id, mode=[INDEX|MANIFEST|STREAM]) and the runtime picks based on source type + heuristics.
Marta Gomez: yes, but keep the API minimal. don't expose too many knobs to user. default should be autodetect with override for edge cases.
Ava Chen: +1. proposal: runtime autodetects source_kind (s3, gcs, local, tarball), runs a cheap probe, picks INDEX for s3/gcs, MANIFEST for tarballs, STREAM for remote RPC-only bundles. config example:
```
warmstart:
  mode: auto
  inflight_prefetch_limit: 3
  prefer_small_shards: true
  size_penalty: 0.8
```
Marta Gomez: testing note: include a perf harness that can replay trace of access_freq per token position. we need both microbench (latency) and macro (throughput + cost) metrics.
Ravi Patel: also think about safety: safetensors index mismatch errors. add checksum validation for index->shard mapping and a graceful fallback path: log, mark shard as bad, try alternate replica.
Ava Chen: good call. will add checksum and health probe. also plan for metrics: prefetch_hit_rate, prefetch_failures, avg_prefetch_latency.
Jin Park: minor: record which shards were primed for each request id to help debugging (trace id -> shard list)
Ava Chen: ok action items: 1) I'll draft an RFC with the scoring fn + API surface, 2) @Ravi + @Marta please review perf test plan, 3) Jin can help add tracing hooks. aiming EOW for RFC draft. :rocket:
Ravi Patel: I'll review. EOD ping if anything is unclear. :+1:
Marta Gomez: will add mem safety tests to the harness. will try to reproduce rtx-pool OOM and capture heap profiles.
infra-bot: reminder: scheduled maint tonight 02:00 UTC — might affect benchmark access to object store

title: #architecture: 2123456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick follow-up from last week's multi-region sync — I'm proposing we add a lightweight 'x-region-stamp' header plus a control-plane residency policy endpoint so edge proxies can do fast local-first routing without holding heavy locks or leases. Thoughts?

Matt Johnson: curious — how is this different from our lease swap flow? seems like duplication.

Ava Chen: Good call. The lease swap is still necessary for shard ownership. This stamp is for per-request routing hints for stateless proxies and CDN-like edges where we want best-effort locality and fast fallback. TTL-based, cryptographically signed, no long-lived ownership. :thinking:

Sophia Patel: Love the idea. Example header sketch:
```
x-region-stamp: region=us-west-2; tenant=acme; exp=1712345600; sig=MEFa...
```
Proxies validate sig and expiry, then prefer local pool if policy allows.

Kai Nguyen: How do we prevent someone spoofing the header and bypassing residency controls?

Ava Chen: Stamp is issued by control-plane KMS per-org and signed with an HMAC/ECDSA key. Edge runtime caches public keys and rejects stamps not verifiable or expired. We'll also reject stamps for tenants flagged as "legal_hold" in policy.

Matt Johnson: When policy says "local-only" (regulatory), do we still route to other regions when local pool is saturated?

Sophia Patel: No. Policy table will have enforcement levels: local-only, local-preferred, any. local-only => route only to resident region; if capacity exhausted, return 429/error or queue depending on tenant SLA. local-preferred => attempt local, fallback to non-resident if stamp expired or explicitly allowed.

Tanvi Rao: Rollout plan idea: emit stamps from control-plane on auth/token exchange and on dedicated handshake. Stamps short-lived (~30s-2m) so failover happens quickly without manual lease revokes. Also add a flag in policy: preserve-residency-for-legal-hold = true to force local-only even during emergency.

Sophia Patel: Minor implementation note: routers will need a small verification cache and a fast path when header absent (treat as default policy lookup by tenant). We'll add a token parser that returns {region, tenant, exp, sig} and a boolean verified.

Ava Chen: Security/threats: Kai -> can you draft a short threat model focusing on stamp replay, key compromise, and cross-tenant spoofing?

Kai Nguyen: On it. Quick suggestions: include per-tenant key rotation, nonce or per-issue counter to detect reuse in high-value flows, and KMS audit hooks.

Matt Johnson: Migration concerns: how do we transition existing proxies? we can't flip all at once.

Ava Chen: Plan: 1) implement stamp-verify as opt-in behind config flag; 2) deploy parser and metrics; 3) enable for a small cohort of tenants with local-preferred; 4) enable enforcement mode for local-only tenants only after we validate metrics. I'll write RFC with rollout steps.

Sophia Patel: Also propose an API on control-plane: /residency/policy?tenant=acme returns one-line policy + signing key id. Keep it tiny to avoid coupling.

Tanvi Rao: For analytics, emit tags: stamp_verified, stamp_region, stamp_policy_level. That helps us measure cost/regional hit-rate. :chart_with_upwards_trend:

router-bot: deploy queued for branch feat/region-stamp-parser on staging (build 4837) :rocket:

Ava Chen: Action items: Ava -> draft RFC + sample header spec; Sophia -> prototype parser + metrics; Kai -> threat model; Matt -> migration checklist + SLA implications; Tanvi -> observability triggers. I'll schedule a short sync next Tues. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

Kai Nguyen: How do we prevent someone spoofing the header and bypassing residency controls?

Ava Chen: Stamp is issued by control-plane KMS per-org and signed with an HMAC/ECDSA key. Edge runtime caches public keys and rejects stamps not verifiable or expired. We'll also reject stamps for tenants flagged as "legal_hold" in policy.

Matt Johnson: When policy says "local-only" (regulatory), do we still route to other regions when local pool is saturated?

Sophia Patel: No. Policy table will have enforcement levels: local-only, local-preferred, any. local-only => route only to resident region; if capacity exhausted, return 429/error or queue depending on tenant SLA. local-preferred => attempt local, fallback to non-resident if stamp expired or explicitly allowed.

Tanvi Rao: Rollout plan idea: emit stamps from control-plane on auth/token exchange and on dedicated handshake. Stamps short-lived (~30s-2m) so failover happens quickly without manual lease revokes. Also add a flag in policy: preserve-residency-for-legal-hold = true to force local-only even during emergency.

Sophia Patel: Minor implementation note: routers will need a small verification cache and a fast path when header absent (treat as default policy lookup by tenant). We'll add a token parser that returns {region, tenant, exp, sig} and a boolean verified.

Ava Chen: Security/threats: Kai -> can you draft a short threat model focusing on stamp replay, key compromise, and cross-tenant spoofing?

Kai Nguyen: On it. Quick suggestions: include per-tenant key rotation, nonce or per-issue counter to detect reuse in high-value flows, and KMS audit hooks.

Matt Johnson: Migration concerns: how do we transition existing proxies? we can't flip all at once.

Ava Chen: Plan: 1) implement stamp-verify as opt-in behind config flag; 2) deploy parser and metrics; 3) enable for a small cohort of tenants with local-preferred; 4) enable enforcement mode for local-only tenants only after we validate metrics. I'll write RFC with rollout steps.

Sophia Patel: Also propose an API on control-plane: /residency/policy?tenant=acme returns one-line policy + signing key id. Keep it tiny to avoid coupling.

Tanvi Rao: For analytics, emit tags: stamp_verified, stamp_region, stamp_policy_level. That helps us measure cost/regional hit-rate. :chart_with_upwards_trend:

router-bot: deploy queued for branch feat/region-stamp-parser on staging (build 4837) :rocket:

Ava Chen: Action items: Ava -> draft RFC + sample header spec; Sophia -> prototype parser + metrics; Kai -> threat model; Matt -> migration checklist + SLA implications; Tanvi -> observability triggers. I'll schedule a short sync next Tues. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-runtime: 2122223333
preview:
Ava Chen: FYI I rolled the updated kernel-selection heuristic to a 10% canary this morning and were seeing a p95/p99 latency climb on gen-rt hosts. Anyone on call?
Ravi Patel: On it — whats the delta, numbers?
Ava Chen: Quick numbers from dashboards: before vs after on mixed workloads (1k token tails):
```
before: p95=72ms p99=72ms
after:  p95=110ms p99=210ms
hosts: gen-rt-*-pool
trace: trace_id=abc123
```
Marta Lopez: Timeline? who deployed? rollout percent?
Ava Chen: Canary at 06:12 UTC (10%). Full rollout was scheduled for 08:00 but I paused when alerts tripped.
deploy-bot: Canary rollout paused at 08:05 by @Ava Chen — rollback available: `/deploy rollback kernel-selector:v3`
Ravi Patel: Can we bisect kernel choices? I suspect an interaction between KV prefetch behavior and the fused kernel choice at batch boundaries. Can someone run a perf profile on an affected host?
Noah Kim: Running `./perf/profile --target gen-rt-12 --samples 200` now. Will paste top flame lines when done.
Noah Kim: Perf flame (top):
```
1. flash_variant_x         34%  (hot path)
2. fused_attn_dispatch     18%
3. paged_attn_kernel       16%
4. kv_prefetch_wait        12%
```
Marta Lopez: Seeing similar on 3 hosts. Batching heuristics appear to pack mixed-length requests such that the fused preference flips mid-batch; when that happens KV cache is often cold and we fall through to paged path.
Ava Chen: Hypothesis: new selector aggressively prefers fused-flash for mixed batches; on seq-length transitions the KV cache misses and we incur paged-attn tail latency. Suggest temporary config: `stable_fallback=true` to prefer fallback kernel until KV warms.
Ravi Patel: Quick test: set env `KERNEL_SELECTOR_STRICT=0` and `KERNEL_FALLBACK_THRESHOLD=0.6` on canary, re-run perf-check.
perf-bot: Perf-check job queued: job_id=perf-21222-01 target=gen-rt-canary (ETA ~8m)
Ava Chen: If perf-check shows improvement we'll either revert the selector commit or tune heuristics. For now leave canary paused.
Noah Kim: Perf job finished: p95 back to 75ms, p99=85ms. Raw: `p95=75 p99=85 samples=600` — looks promising.
Marta Lopez: Confirmed across 3 canary hosts. Root cause looks like the interaction: aggressive fused preference + tight batch boundary heuristics.
Ravi Patel: I'll open a bisect/tweak PR to lower fused preference default to 0.4 and add a guard in KV prefetch logic to avoid prefetching on boundary <=2. Tagging @eng-runtime for review.
Ava Chen: Thanks. Please add a stress integration test (mixing seq lengths) and document rollback steps in the release notes.
Noah Kim: Rolling rollback on canary to `v2-kselector` now.
deploy-bot: Rollback complete on canary at 09:12 UTC — statuses OK.
Marta Lopez: Follow-up: schedule slower rollout of the kernel selector behind a feature flag and add a perf guard: if p99 rises >25% over baseline within 1h abort rollout.
Ava Chen: :thumbsup: Good plan. Let's sync tomorrow for deeper perf triage and then re-evaluate the selector after tests.

title: #eng-runtime: 2105012345
preview:
Ava Chen: heads-up — canary runs on int8-quantized Llama-3 variant show a slow, steady memory increase on long-token sessions ( > 50k tokens). Not a sharp leak, more like a slope. Seen on two different GPU SKUs.
Sam Ortiz: can you paste the profiler slice? memory slope sounds like either KV residency not dropping or fragmentation causing free-list starvation.
Ava Chen: putting a small dump. profiler snapshot at t=75k tokens:
```
KV: total_slots=1_200_000 used_slots=742_312 free_slots=457_688
slab_frag_bytes=34_432_768 free_list_len=3
page_allocs=11245 page_evictions=52
mem_total_gb=28.9 mem_used_gb=23.6 mem_slope_mb_per_min=3.2
quant_mode=INT8 kernel=fast_attn_v3 batch_heuristic=adaptive_lowlat
```
Ava Chen: note slab_frag_bytes >> expected (~8MB baseline). mem_slope for BF16 runs is ~0.7 MB/min under same load.
Liam Patel: suspicious: fast_attn_v3 has a different kv-page alignment/padding. INT8 path seems to allocate slightly wider KV pages (padding to 256B) which can multiply fragmentation when many small entries exist.
Maya Rios: i ran a short AB test switching kernel to fast_attn_v2 + int8, slope dropped to ~1.1 MB/min and slab_frag_bytes ~9MB. Accuracy hit was tiny but reproducible: rouge-ish metrics show -0.6% after sustained long sessions.
Sam Ortiz: can you share per-request KV churn? if the batching heuristic is switching to smaller batches for low-latency, that increases KV write frequency which interacts badly with fragmentation.
Ava Chen: here's an excerpt from request-level telemetry (4min window):
```
reqs=420 avg_batch_size=3 median_seq_len=512 writes_per_min=1_200 kv_writes=18_900
avg_kv_entry_size=184 bytes hot_prefix_hit=0.23
```
Ava Chen: writes_per_min high because adaptive_lowlat flips to per-request flush for sequences > 256, leading to more frequent partial page fills.
Sam Ortiz: ok that would explain free_list_len drop. free list of 3 means allocs fall back to new pages instead of reusing freed fragments.
Maya Rios: accuracy delta note: INT8 impact is correlated with which KV pages get evicted vs paged — when a hot block with mixed-precision keys gets compacted we saw a ~0.8% top1 perplexity bump for short windows after eviction. It recovers after warmup.
Liam Patel: hypothesis: two factors compounding — (1) kernel's allocation alignment pads small entries causing fragmentation; (2) batching heuristic increasing write frequency creating many small partially-filled pages. Combined leads to gradual mem growth.
perf-bot: alert: kv_mem_slope > 2 MB/min for 30m (canary-fleet: int8-fast_attn_v3). Pager: eng-runtime on call.
Ava Chen: proposed experiments:
- run int8 + fast_attn_v2 for 24h canary (we did a short run, extend it)
- run int8 + fast_attn_v3 but force batch_heuristic=throughput (bigger batches)
- instrument allocator to expose per-slab free-list distribution and reclamation latency
- add a metric: kv_fragmentation_ratio = slab_frag_bytes / mem_used
Maya Rios: I've already started the 24h int8+v2 canary. Link to heatmap and perf dashboard: https://console.redwood.ai/kvm/heatmaps/longrun/abcd-123
Sam Ortiz: quick mitigation we can ship: conservative change to allocator to prefer coalescing small frees into a reclaimable bucket when free_list_len < 16. Also throttle per-request KV flush when avg_batch_size < 4 (to prefer coalescing).
Liam Patel: coalescing will add CPU cost — need to measure kernel selection tradeoffs. If we coalesce aggressively, int8-v3 might lose its low-latency benefit. We should quantify: added CPU ~+6% for coalescing loop.
Ava Chen: actionables agreed:
1) extend Maya's 24h run (Maya)
2) add allocator telemetry and kv_fragmentation_ratio (Sam)
3) run int8+v3 with throughput batching for 8h to see slope change (Ava)
4) prototype coalescing patch behind a feature flag and measure latency delta (Liam)
5) open tracking issue with perf trace and sample dumps (Ava -> will create)
Sam Ortiz: also flag for eval team: add a regression set that includes long-session prompts to catch post-warmup accuracy drift (Maya can coordinate)
Maya Rios: noted, i'll add 3 prompt tracks: synthetic long chat, real customer long-draft, and iterative summarization.
Ava Chen: FYI, here's the repro command I used for the short run:
```
./canary_runner --model llama3-int8 --kernel fast_attn_v3 --batch-heuristic adaptive_lowlat --session-length 100000 --emit-telemetry
```
Liam Patel: one more insight from local sim: if we force page_size=4k vs 16k padding, frag_bytes halves. Kernel's buffer alignment macro was rounding up to 16k for int8 path for vectorization reasons.
Sam Ortiz: if that alignment is the culprit we can try smaller vector width kernels or add a per-arch override. But that's deeper change — try allocator mitigations first.
perf-bot: note: created incident-xxx with logs and the initial profiler snapshot.
Ava Chen: thanks all — i'll file the issue and paste links. keeping this thread as the single source for the canary observations and action list. :thumbsup:

title: #customer-success: 1998401234
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick weekly sync for Acme — noticed a model-mix shift after Friday's rollout. High-level: usage +23% W/W, requests 126k -> 155k, avg spend +9%. Fallbacks spiked and latency hit a 95p of ~780ms at peak. Posting details and next steps below.

Diego Martinez: When exactly was the rollout and what traffic split?

Ava Chen: Fri 11:14 UTC start (canary 20%), reached 100% by 11:40 UTC. Fallback surge started ~12:10 UTC and coincided with a short customer traffic burst.

deploy-bot: Deployment event — redwood-open-v2 rollout -> 100% at 2026-03-19T11:40:12Z :rocket:

Kai Patel: Pulled the telemetry. Key numbers: fallback rate baseline 0.9% -> peak 4.1% (15:12 minute). 95th latency for text-gen increased from 420ms -> 780ms during the spike. Error types: kvcache-miss, attention OOM.

Diego Martinez: Can you paste example error traces / logs so infra can triage quicker?

Ava Chen: copy of a couple lines from logs: `ERROR: kvcache-miss key=usr:acme:session:49f2` and `FATAL: attention_kernel OOM at step=23 model=redwood-open-v2 shards=2`. Also seeing a few `429 fallback` counts.

Olivia Park: (Acme PM) we shipped the bulk-chat feature Friday morning — sessions now include 900–1,800 token histories often. We expected more token consumption but not the degradation. Can you advise if there's a quick mitigation? :grimacing:

Diego Martinez: Short-term options: route long-context sessions to high-memory pool, reduce `max_tokens` for server-side completions, or toggle a smaller quantized variant. Proposed temporary routing rule: 
```
route-rule: if avg_tokens > 512 -> redwood-compat-highmem
else -> redwood-open-v2
```
This should reduce OOMs while we tune caching.

Kai Patel: Cost impact estimate for Friday peak: compute +9% (~$1.7k extra/day @ current unit rates). If we enable prefix/KV caching for ~30% of sessions we estimate fallback down ~1.2pp and cost reduction ~4%. I'll share the numbers on the dashboard.

Ava Chen: Action items I suggest: 1) enable routing rule (Diego) 2) infra to bump highmem pool temporarily (SRE) 3) enable prefix cache for Acme (Kai) 4) customer enablement to nudge prompt trimming where possible (Olivia). I'll coordinate the call and follow-ups.

Diego Martinez: I'll open a Dedicated ticket for pool adjustment and add a canary rollback plan in case the fix regresses latency. :thumbsup:

Olivia Park: Thanks — can we do a 30m call with Acme PMs tomorrow morning PT? They are most available 9am PT.

Ava Chen: Scheduled tentative sync: 2026-03-20 16:00 UTC (9am PT). I'll send the invite and include Diego + Kai.

Kai Patel: Working on a compact dashboard with the spike graphs and token distribution, will drop link here in ~30m.

Ava Chen: Great — please add the estimated cost delta and fallback trend. I'll update this thread after the call with outcomes. Thanks everyone. :pray:

title: #customer-success: 1963001123
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick capture from HelixCardio onboarding retro earlier today — writing up raw asks so we don't lose anything. TL;DR customer is happy with latency but running into visibility + control gaps.

Ava Chen: Raw asks (verbatim / summarized):
- "per-route cost breakdown per model" — they want to see which route is burning budget.
- "granular RBAC for routes and models" — legal wants only certain teams to call prod models.
- "retry/debug logs on streaming failures" — hard to triage intermittent disconnects.
- "embedding drift detection" — embeddings for search are degrading after model swap.
- "model fallback tunables" — want to control when we fallback to cheaper variants.
- "onboarding docs: quickstart for dedicated customers" — missing checklist for infra/network steps.

Miguel Torres: Nice summary. Can we prioritize 1-2 for Q2? Renewal convo next week, want a list to promise if we surface as committed work. :eyes:

Jordan Patel: Suggest we do an impact x effort quick matrix. My initial read: cost breakdown = high impact / med effort; RBAC = high impact / high effort; retry logs = med impact / low effort; embedding drift = high impact / med/high effort; fallback tunables = high impact / low-med effort; docs = low effort / med impact.

Priya Singh: Adding direct customer quotes (for context):
- "We can't justify volume until we can show where the spend goes."
- "Our security team will not accept broad keys for model calls."

Liam O'Riley: From eng POV: retry/debug logs and fallback tunables look like the fastest wins. Retry logs -> add structured events + request_id propagation, ~2 sprints. Fallback tunables -> config + route-level policy, ~1 sprint. Per-route cost needs backend work + ui, ~3-4 sprints.

Jordan Patel: OK. Proposal: mark 'per-route cost' and 'RBAC' as required for renewal (if we can't commit, we escalate). But target delivery: fallback tunables + retry logs as Q2 quickships. Can someone own triage + ticketing?

Ava Chen: I'll own the triage for CS side — will convert customer notes into acceptance criteria and hand to Jordan for PM prioritization.

Jordan Patel: I'll create tickets and add labels. Draft tickets being created: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/RED-4512 (fallback-tunables), https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/RED-4513 (retry-logs). Will add priority and link to this thread.

cs-bot: Action logged: "create:triage_note" by Ava Chen on 1963001123

Miguel Torres: Please add an "renewal_risk" flag on the per-route-cost item — we need that surfaced in the renewal tracker.

Ava Chen: Done. Updated triage note with renewal_risk=high and customer severity examples.

Jordan Patel: Quick checklist I'll add to each ticket (copy/paste):
```
- customer_impact: <quote>
- acceptance_criteria: <what we will demo>
- owner: <name>
- estimate: <sprint points>
- renewal_risk_flag: <high/med/low>
```

Priya Singh: Also adding: copy of onboarding notes and the customer's recommended rollout plan (they prefer canary on dedicated first). I'll drop the doc in the CS folder.

Liam O'Riley: If we go with canary for fallback tunables, we should add metrics to alert on quality regressions. Adding to ticket.

Ava Chen: Thanks team. Next steps: Jordan to add tickets + labels; Liam to add engineering estimates; Ava to push triage note and schedule a 30m sync with HelixCardio if Jordan flags we can commit.

Miguel Torres: :thumbsup: — please ping me once tickets are live; I'm on the renewal call Thurs.

Jordan Patel: Will do.

Ava Chen: Ending thread, will update when docs/tickets exist.
